stem: From the top of a 35-foot watchtower, a ranger spots a campfire at an angle of depression θ, where sin θ = 5/13, cos θ = 12/13, and tan θ = 5/12. A firebreak is located 36 feet directly beyond the campfire (farther from the tower), at the same ground level. The watchtower has a rotating searchlight. What is the straight-line distance from the top of the watchtower to the firebreak, in feet?
explanation: First, find the horizontal distance from the tower to the campfire. Using tan θ = opposite/adjacent = 35/d, so d = 35/tan θ = 35/(5/12) = 35 × (12/5) = 84 feet. The firebreak is 36 feet beyond the campfire, so it is 84 + 36 = 120 feet from the tower horizontally. The straight-line distance from the tower top to the firebreak is √(120² + 35²) = √(14400 + 1225) = √15625 = 125 feet. The searchlight information is not needed.

distractorRationale.A: Found the straight-line distance to the campfire instead of the firebreak, forgetting the 36-foot offset: √(84² + 35²) = √(7056 + 1225) = √8281 = 91.
distractorRationale.B: Correctly found the horizontal distance to the firebreak (84 + 36 = 120) but reported it as the answer without accounting for the 35-foot tower height = 120.
distractorRationale.C: Correct: horizontal to fire = 84, to firebreak = 120, distance = √(120² + 35²) = √15625 = 125.
distractorRationale.D: Added the horizontal and vertical distances instead of using the Pythagorean theorem: 120 + 35 = 155.

imageDescription: A side view showing a 35-foot watchtower on the left. From the top, a dashed line angles down at angle θ to a campfire on the ground 84 feet from the tower base. A firebreak is marked 36 feet farther from the tower at 120 feet total. A solid line shows the straight-line distance from the tower top to the firebreak, forming the hypotenuse of a right triangle with the 35-foot height and 120-foot horizontal.
